application_text:
Show HN launch: Show HN: LectureToBook – Turn Videos into a PDF/ePub

URL: https://lecturetobook.com/

Story: Lecturetobook takes a video presentation and turns it into an editable document, with photos, diagrams, etc. extracted from your slides. I came up with this idea because I was enrolled in an online course and it was all videos. I spend all day long on the computer for work, so the last thing I want to do after work is spend more time staring at a screen. I wanted something that could take those videos and turn them into a readable format. The most difficult part was detecting what frames in the video are slides. It uses an AI model to extract what is useful from the slides. Feedback welcome! This is my first side-project that has made it to launch.
screen_reason: The application clearly describes the product as a tool that converts video presentations into editable documents.

hits.title: Show HN: LectureToBook – Turn Videos into a PDF/ePub
hits.author: h02
hits.story_text:
Lecturetobook takes a video presentation and turns it into an editable document, with photos, diagrams, etc. extracted from your slides.

I came up with this idea because I was enrolled in an online course and it was all videos. I spend all day long on the computer for work, so the last thing I want to do after work is spend more time staring at a screen. I wanted something that could take those videos and turn them into a readable format.

The most difficult part was detecting what frames in the video are slides, and extracting from those what is useful content to add to the document. I didn't want to just stick a screengrab of the slides in the text, I wanted it to read like an actual book, so it uses an AI model to take from the slides what is useful and ignores what is just repeated in the transcript.

Here is an output sample from an NIH video: https://lecturetobook.com/sample/sleep-book/book.html

Feedback welcome! This is my first side-project that has made it to launch.

results.title: Founder Profile: Prateek Joshi — Founder & CEO, Pluto AI - Imagine H2O
results.content: # Founder Profile: Prateek Joshi — Founder & CEO, Pluto AI. We always get excited when entrepreneurs from outside the sector choose to focus their talents on water issues. Prateek Joshi of Pluto AI is a perfect example. After starting his career at Qualcomm and NVidia, Prateek is now an author of multiple books and a regular speaker on AI and Machine Learning, and has turned his considerable talents to helping utilities get the most out of their ever-expanding trove of data. **Give us an overview of your role, what your experiences are and what you are working on.**. I’m Prateek Joshi, founder of Pluto AI, an analytics platform for water and wastewater plants to predict asset performance, reduce energy consumption, and minimize operating costs. In order to solve these issues, they have to extract intelligence from this unstructured data scattered across multiple sources. **What’s the impact you want to have on the water industry?**.

results.title: World Book Day with Nadia Odunayo Founder of The StoryGraph > Women Who Code
results.content: # World Book Day with Nadia Odunayo Founder of The StoryGraph. Nadia Odunayo is the founder of The StoryGraph, where you find books to fit your mood. After you read the interview, remember to head over to The StoryGraph, to find your next read. One of the reasons I need glasses/contact lenses now is because I spent a lot of time reading books with a torch under the covers when I was supposed to be sleeping! **The StoryGraph solves the problem "what am I in the mood to read," can you share how your love for books and experience as a software engineer led to translating the StoryGraph publication into the most customized reading tool available today?**. I had the most fun I’d ever had coding and I realized that a big part of it was that I, as a reader, was so excited to use the tool I was building myself.

No backend knowledge, barely any JavaScript. Just described what she wanted and iterated until it worked just to showcase her thought process through prototypes than using Figma.

> If you need a better understanding for which vibe coding platform to use in 2026, read this: [...] Lack of strong typing leads to runtime errors.

Zod handles schema validation and type safety in TypeScript/JavaScript, while Pydantic does the same for Python.

They catch errors at compile time, making code more robust and AI integrations smoother.

In 2026, they provide runtime validation for safer apps, with Zod v4 adding type mismatches checks. Pydantic is like Zod for Python, enabling type-safe models and JSON schemas. They enforce outputs in AI agents and validate API responses.

results.title: 10 Developer Tools I Replaced in 2026 (and Why)
results.content:
TablePlus is a native Mac/Windows/Linux app with tabs, inline editing, and support for 15+ databases including Postgres, MySQL, SQLite, Redis, and MongoDB. $79 one-time license per seat (or free with a two-tab limit that’s surprisingly usable for quick lookups). Over 100,000 developers use it, including teams at Microsoft and Amazon.

The other contender is Drizzle Studio if you’re already using Drizzle ORM (34,000+ GitHub stars). It spins up a visual interface directly from your schema definition, so you don’t need a separate app at all. Run `drizzle-kit studio` and your database browser materializes from your TypeScript types. For Drizzle users, it’s the obvious choice. For everyone else, TablePlus.

`drizzle-kit studio`

## The Pattern Behind the Switches [...] ## 4. Postman → Hoppscotch

It’s almost embarrassing how long developers keep Postman installed. The app had gotten bloated, the account requirement was annoying, and every update seemed to add features I didn’t ask for while the core “send a request and see what happens” experience got slower. One developer on Reddit put it well: “Postman started feeling like enterprise software when I just needed to check a response header.”

Hoppscotch is open-source (MIT), has 79,000+ GitHub stars, runs in the browser, needs no account, and does the thing I actually need: send HTTP requests, inspect responses, manage collections. REST, GraphQL, gRPC. It loads in under a second. The self-hosted option means your API collections n

results.title: 10 Best Developer Tools for 2026 Tech Stack
results.content:
The 2026 standout feature is Seer, Sentry’s AI engine. It automatically groups similar errors, suggests root causes, and generates fix recommendations based on your codebase context. AI Code Review scans commits for potential issues before they reach production. Session Replay records exactly what users saw before an error occurred, replacing the “can you reproduce it?” conversation with a video.

Performance monitoring tracks database queries, API calls, and rendering times across your full stack. The `Next.js` integration is particularly deep, with automatic instrumentation for Server Components and API routes. For LLM and agent workloads specifically, AI API analytics for agent traffic covers token-level cost tracking and multi-step trace tools that traditional APM misses.

`Next.js` [...] ## Picking the Right Stack

There’s no single correct combination, but there are patterns that work. For a `Next.js` MVP targeting web users: Cursor + Vercel + Supabase + shadcn/ui + Playwright + Linear. For a mobile-first app: Cursor + Firebase + React Native + Playwright + Sentry. For a solo founder validating an idea: Replit + Supabase + Vercel.

`Next.js`

These tools share a common trait: they reduce the distance between idea and deployed product. A two-person team can now ship what used to require ten. Pick the stack that matches your product’s constraints, not the one with the most features. For more options, check our web app frameworks guide and cloud hosting comparison. For too

results.title: Best Developer Browser Extensions for Productivity in 2026 | daily.dev
results.content:
A few trends emerge from this comparison. Debugging tools like React DevTools, Vue DevTools, and axe DevTools provide consistent cross-browser functionality, making them versatile choices for developers regardless of their browser preference. On the other hand, workflow-oriented tools such as Postman Interceptor lean heavily toward Chrome, leaving Firefox users with fewer options.

Among these, daily.dev is unique because it integrates seamlessly into a developer's daily routine rather than being a situational tool. Its AI-powered feed, free features, and 4.8/5 Chrome Web Store rating make it an appealing choice for staying updated, especially since it doesn’t track your browsing history or alter other pages. Firefox users can access similar functionality through its PWA version. [...] Advertisers  Recruiters

results.title: Best Developer Productivity Tools 2026
results.content:
### Agents Need Codebase Context to Make Good Changes

Agents are only as good as their context. A coding agent that understands the current file can make local edits. A coding agent that understands the codebase can make better cross-file changes.

This is why tools with strong codebase context matter. Sourcegraph helps humans and agents understand large codebases. Greptile reviews PRs against the whole repo. Cursor, Claude Code, Windsurf, Codex, and Augment all compete partly on how well they can gather and use context.

### Costs Can Be Hard to Predict

Agentic coding can be more expensive than autocomplete. A small-sounding task may require many file reads, model calls, retries, test runs, and context windows. [...] The mistake is treating all AI tools for developers as interchangeable. GitHub Copilot, Greptile, Cursor, Sentry Seer, and Snyk Code all improve developer productivity, but they solve different problems.

### AI Developer Tools Are the Fastest-Growing Part of the Category [...] Introducing TREX: Greptile Now Runs Your Code.TREX Runs Your Code.
